data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1451 ET - Fair Isaac Corp. is still looking to raise prices on its mortgage credit-score metric despite rising competition. Mortgage brokers are now allowed to use the competing metric VantageScore to measure applicants' creditworthiness, thanks to a new federal rule. FICO CEO William Lansing tells analysts there is a gap between the value of the score and its pricing, which has increased eight-fold in the past five years. "We're always looking at how we're going to close that gap," he says. Lansing says FICO can manage the new competition because it already competes with VantageScore in non-mortgage lending. "We continue to be committed to that philosophy on price change," he says. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1450 ET - Newell Brands slides 16% after cutting its FY2025 EPS outlook, but CEO Christopher Peterson says the household products maker is making progress on a turnaround strategy. "With second quarter results now posted, first half core sales for 2025 came in at minus 3.4%, which is an improvement versus a year ago, but does interrupt the steady sequential progress that had been delivered during each six-month period up until now," Peterson says on an analyst call. He says core sales have dramatically improved each six-month period since outlining its new corporate strategy in June 2023: going from down 14.7% in the first half of 2023 to down 2.3% in the second half of 2024. He adds that core sales softness this quarter was largely driven by category weakness related to consumers pulling back and retailer actions. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1426 ET - Kimberly-Clark's efforts to adapt some of its best product features for its value tier are starting to pay off, CEO Mike Hsu says during a call with analysts. The company saw organic sales up 3.9% in its latest quarter, driven primarily by demand for new products, he says. Going forward, the company plans to bring its innovation efforts to the premium and mid-tier segments, which should drive further growth into the back half of the year, he says. "The consumption is very durable in our categories, and that's really driven by excellent innovation and excellent execution," he says. Kimberly-Clark up 4.5%. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1151 ET - Colgate-Palmolive is embarking on restructuring that it says will in part streamline its organizational structure and optimize its global supply chain. Under the so-called productivity program, the maker of Ajax dish soap and Speed Stick deodorants will also invest more heavily in emerging technologies like artificial intelligence. "AI will be a difference maker for us in our revenue-growth management efforts, as we work with our retail partners to use data analytics and machine learning to optimize our portfolio and promotional spending to solve for the best combination of sales and profit growth," CEO Noel Wallace says on a call with analysts. Colgate expects to record $200 million to $300 million in charges over the next three years as a result of the program. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1133 ET - In order to hit its sales targets, Colgate-Palmolive says it will need to raise prices and sell more of its higher-end products, without losing too many customers as a result. "The environment remains difficult with category volatility; geopolitical, macroeconomic and consumer uncertainty; high raw-material and packaging costs, including as a result of tariffs; and lower levels of in-market inflation," CEO Noel Wallace says on a call with analysts. He notes that, while the consumer-products maker may have to switch tactics depending on short-term fluctuations in the market, its long-term goals haven't changed. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1128 ET — Apple is starting to spend on AI but it's way behind its tech competitors. Apple has so far invested $10 billion on AI, compared with $130 billion, $120 billion and $85 billion at Microsoft, Amazon and Google respectively, Quilter Cheviot analyst Ben Barringer says. Apple said during its earnings call Thursday it would substantially increase capital expenditures in the year ahead. While it's unlikely to match its competitors, increasing spending would be a positive sign that Apple is preparing to be more aggressive about its future capabilities, Barringer says. The company also hasn't ruled out mergers and acquisitions, which could help it catch up, Barringer adds. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1126 ET - Colgate-Palmolive CEO Noel Wallace says the maker of Irish Spring soaps and Cuddly fabric softeners is actively leveraging price-pack architecture to deliver consumer perceived value. "This can be through larger-size, multi-packs, where consumers pay a lower price per usage, or through smaller sizes for consumers who are looking for a lower out-of-pocket expense," he says on a call with analysts. Moving forward, Wallace says the company will leverage its global supply chain's breadth, resiliency and agility to respond to changes in consumer preference. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1121 ET - Amazon didn't explicitly say AWS could accelerate in 3Q but analysts at BofA Securities think it can. The internet retailer's cloud-computing arm reported sales climbed 17.5%, which is weighing on its stock given lofty investor expectations. The analysts say that Amazon is still capacity constrained and growing capex. They also point out that AWS added the second-most revenue dollars quarter-over-quarter in its history despite these constraints. That occurred as its backlog grew $6 billion quarter-over-quarter, which is below peers, but year-over-year growth accelerated to 25%. "AWS lost ground to Azure in 2Q, but Cloud growth can be lumpy," the analysts say. "Though AWS is not seeing the benefit of ChatGPT usage, broader enterprise GenAI adoption remains a growth catalyst." Shares decline 7.6% to $216.23. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1113 ET - Gold futures rise after U.S. economic data came in softer than expected. Futures are up 1.6% at $3,402.30 a troy ounce. Nonfarm Payroll data showed just 73,000 jobs were added in July, below analyst expectations of 100,000. Even more suprising, revisions to May and June's data revised jobs down by a huge 258,000, ING analysts say in a note. This highlights that the jobs market has lost momentum even earlier than thought and will intensify pressure from President Trump for the Federal Reserve to loosen monetary policy, ING says. With tariffs set to eat into household spending and corporate profits, the case for cutting interest rates has been significantly strengthened, ING says. Lower interest rates typically benefit non-interest bearing bullion. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

1109 ET - Shares in Watches of Switzerland plunge following the latest news on President Trump's tariffs on Swiss products. The U.S. unveiled plans to impose a 39% tariff rate on goods imports from Switzerland, effective Aug. 7. The new rate is higher than the 31% announced on April 2. The U.K. luxury-watch retailer, which sells brands including Rolex, has a strong presence in the U.S. The country accounts for about 48% of its sales, according to its latest annual report. The stock trades 7.6% lower. (andrea.figueras@wsj.com)

1104 ET - Palantir's $10 billion, 10-year contract with the U.S. Army sees the software company continuing to attach itself to safe, high-priority programs and contracts with the Defense Department that are not at risk of getting cut in the current government-spending climate, Wedbush analysts say in a research note. The company is well positioned in this spending environment and poised to actually get more of the Pentagon's information-technology budget dollars, the analysts say. This new deal should be a positive growth catalyst and give Palantir a bigger seat at the table globally, the analysts say. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1053 ET - Overall, Imperial Oil had a good quarter, reckons Raymond James's Michael Barth. The analyst says the energy company managed a modest 4% beat on funds from operations over consensus estimates on what largely appears to be slightly stronger production. Still, given that Imperial is one of the best performing large-cap Canadian energy names, Barth is skeptical the latest quarter will be enough to drive meaningful outperformance against its peer group from here. Shares are down 1% at C$114.34. (robb.stewart@wsj.com)

data.title: Big Tech Says Consumers Are Still Spending. Can It Last? — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Sabrina Escobar

Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell said this week there was "no question" consumer spending had cooled in recent months. But if Americans are pulling back on purchases, the country's tech giants haven't felt it yet.

The coming months could determine whether Big Tech can defy an economic slowdown — or fall victim to the same ailments afflicting traditional retailers.

Amazon's net sales in North America rose 11% to $100 billion in the quarter, and are up a solid 9% for the first half of the fiscal year.

"We just haven't seen diminished demand," said Amazon.com CEO Andrew Jassy.

Apple notched record revenue in more than two dozen countries and regions, including the U.S., which saw sales rise 9% year over year. iPhone sales, which had been weak in past quarters, rose 13% globally, reflecting an encouraging renaissance in one of Apple's key revenue drivers.

Other, smaller tech companies also showed no sign of faltering consumers.

eBay had a strong quarter, with U.S. gross merchandise value growth accelerating to 7% in the quarter from nearly 0.5% the prior quarter. The results were driven by stronger-than-expected consumer demand and increases in average selling prices, executives said.

"When you look at the macro environment, US in Q2 was more favorable than we expected despite the tariff announcements and the elimination of the de minimis for imported goods," said eBay CEO Jamie Iannone.

Etsy's Chief Financial Officer Lanny Baker noted the company hadn't seen any big shifts in spending tied to tariffs or trade announcements.

"The general read is the consumer that we're seeing looks a little bit better — slightly better than it was perhaps three months ago with higher income households a little bit healthier within that," Baker said.

Still the upbeat assessments from tech companies seemed to clash with the market's dour mood at the end of a data-heavy week.

The takeaway from several economic releases is that inflation is moving higher while hiring is slowing. The new tariffs announced by President Donald Trump on Thursday — which range from a baseline 10% rate to as much as 40% or 50% for select countries — could push prices up further.

The lingering question is low long consumers will remain resilient.

Until this year, a strong labor market and wage gains supported American spending. Given the unemployment rate remains low at 4.2% and wage growth has remained stable, these tailwinds could continue to undergird spending in the near future.

Indeed, Etsy's Baker said that while the macroeconomic environment remains uncertain, the business had performed well in July, "reflecting sustained healthy consumer trends in the US."

But the consumer outlook beyond that is cloudy, and in a way contingent on how companies respond to the new tariffs. If they start hiking prices once their current inventories sell off, shoppers may cut back even more.

"It's hard to know what will happen when we deplete some of the prebuys that we did on our own first-party retail and then some of the forward deploying that we saw of our third-party selling partners," Amazon's Jassy said. "And when — if costs go up over time — we're unsure at this point who's going to end up absorbing those higher costs."

Of course, even in the toughest economic times, there are winners and losers. Some e-commerce companies are poised to emerge victorious, likely giving them additional confidence.

Amazon's low prices have helped the company gain market share among budget-conscious consumers. Meanwhile, if tariffs push up the cost to import new products, the demand for secondhand goods could increase, benefiting secondhand sellers such as eBay.

The true test still lies ahead, however. Big-box retailers have yet to report their second-quarter results, which could give investors further insight on whether Big Tech's bullish outlook is a fluke, or if it is an accurate reflection of the broader consumer environment.

Write to Sabrina Escobar at sabrina.escobar@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Bitcoin’s Independence Day: 8 years after the Block Size Wars
data.description:
August 1 marks the eighth anniversary of a key moment in Bitcoin’s history — the initial activation of Segregated Witness (SegWit) in 2017, a key software upgrade that reshaped the network’s future and triggered a hard fork.

The move, known as “Bitcoin Independence Day,” reduced miner influence over the protocol and led to the creation of Bitcoin Cash , highlighting the community's divisions over how Bitcoin should scale to support global use.

The “block size wars” were fought between those who wanted to keep the Bitcoin block size small and “big blockers,” a coalition of miners and businesses that wanted to include more transactions in each block to make BTC suitable for everyday payments and commercial transactions.

Big blockers, led by “Bitcoin Jesus,” Roger Ver, argued that Bitcoin fell short of Satoshi Nakamoto’s vision of a peer-to-peer electronic cash system since its ledger’s limited block space could never scale enough to accommodate the world’s transactions.

Node operators, developers and BTC users mounted a strong opposition to the bigger block proposals, arguing that raising the block size would also increase storage requirements for node operators. 

A potential increase in storage requirements would make running a node prohibitive for the average user, thereby centralizing the Bitcoin network in the hands of a few large players who could run the required hardware. 

Bitcoin Improvement Proposal (BIP) 91 was activated in August 2017, paving the way for scaling through the BTC Lightning Network, a way of facilitating offchain payment channels between two or more users, with one final settlement on the Bitcoin ledger.

On Aug. 1, 2017, the big blockers split from the Bitcoin network, resulting in BCH creation and altering Bitcoin history forever.

Bitcoin and BCH, where are they now?

Since the Bitcoin Cash hard fork in August 2017, the price has oscillated, hitting an all-time high of about $1,600 in May 2021 during the previous bull market cycle.

However, the price soon collapsed, hitting an all-time low of about $90 during the 2022 bear market. Currently, BCH is trading at about $552 — the same price levels it was trading at eight years ago, immediately following its debut.

Meanwhile, the price of Bitcoin has appreciated by about 4,200% during that same eight-year period. On Aug. 1, 2017, BTC was trading hands at about $2,718 and is currently trading at about $115,000, down from its all-time high of about $122,000 recorded in July.

Today, Bitcoin boasts a market cap of over $2.2 trillion, while Bitcoin Cash has a total market cap of around $10.9 billion.

The schism between the two networks highlights the debate between those who want to use the BTC network for a variety of purposes, including retail purchases and file storage, versus those defining BTC as a decentralized store of value — a conflict that still rages today.

data.title: SOUTHERN FIRST BANCSHARES INC SEC 10-Q Report
data.description:
Southern First Bancshares Inc., a prominent financial institution known for its client-focused business model, has released its Form 10-Q report for the second quarter of 2025. The report highlights significant financial growth and strategic business expansions, reflecting the company's robust performance and future outlook.

Financial Highlights

  • Total Interest Income: $52.3 million, reflecting a 3.5% increase from the previous year, driven by higher interest income from loans.
  • Total Interest Expense: $27.0 million, a decrease of 12.9% compared to the previous year, primarily due to reduced interest expenses on deposits.
  • Net Interest Income: $25.3 million, a 29.5% increase over the previous year, attributed to a decrease in interest expense and an increase in interest income.
  • Net Income: $6.6 million for the three months ended June 30, 2025, compared to $3.0 million for the same period in 2024, primarily driven by an increase in net interest income.
  • Diluted EPS: $0.81 for the second quarter of 2025, compared to $0.37 for the same period in 2024, reflecting improved profitability.

Business Highlights

  • Client-Focused Business Model: The company continues to emphasize a client-focused business model, utilizing relationship teams to provide clients with a specific banker contact and support team responsible for all their banking needs. This structure aims to deliver a consistent and superior level of professional service, which is considered a critical part of the company's culture, referred to as 'ClientFIRST.'
  • Geographical Expansion: The company is expanding into the Charlotte, North Carolina, Greensboro, North Carolina, and Atlanta, Georgia markets. This expansion is part of the company's strategy to grow its presence in these regions and potentially enter new markets.
  • Loan Portfolio Composition: As of June 30, 2025, the company's loan portfolio is heavily concentrated in real estate loans, which represent 83.2% of total loans. This concentration reflects the company's focus on real estate lending as a significant part of its business operations.
  • Interest Rate Environment Impact: The company is navigating a changing interest rate environment, with the Federal Reserve beginning to lower interest rates in early 2025. This change is expected to support loan demand but may also compress net interest margins.
  • Operational Risk Management: The company is actively managing risks associated with economic and regulatory changes, including interest rate risk, credit risk, and market risk. The company maintains access to multiple sources of liquidity to support its operations and capital ratios.
  • Future Outlook: The company is evaluating the impact of the One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA) on its deferred tax assets and liabilities, effective tax rate, and tax-related processes. Preliminary analysis suggests that the OBBBA will not have a material impact on the company's 2025 estimated annual effective tax rate or consolidated financial statements.

SEC Filing: SOUTHERN FIRST BANCSHARES INC [ SFST ] - 10-Q - Aug. 01, 2025

data.title: METTLER TOLEDO INTERNATIONAL INC/ SEC 10-Q Report
data.description:
Mettler Toledo International Inc., a global leader in precision instruments and services for use in laboratories and manufacturing, has released its Form 10-Q report for the second quarter of 2025. The report highlights the company's financial performance and operational achievements for the three months ended June 30, 2025.

Financial Highlights

Net Sales: The company reported net sales of $983.2 million, marking a 4% increase compared to the same period in 2024. This growth was primarily driven by strong performance in product inspection and process analytics.

Gross Profit: Gross profit for the quarter was $579.9 million, representing 59.0% of net sales. This is a slight decrease from 59.7% in the prior year, mainly due to increased tariff costs.

Earnings Before Taxes: Earnings before taxes were $248.7 million, showing a modest increase from $243.2 million in the previous year, attributed to cost-saving initiatives.

Net Earnings: Net earnings for the quarter were $202.3 million, down from $221.8 million in the prior year. The decrease was influenced by higher tariff costs and lower sales volume.

Diluted Earnings Per Common Share: Diluted earnings per share were $9.76, a decline from $10.37 in the previous year, reflecting the decrease in net earnings.

Business Highlights

Revenue Segments: The company's net sales are divided into three main product categories: laboratory (56% of sales), industrial (39% of sales), and retail (5% of sales). The Swiss Operations are largely comprised of laboratory products, while the Chinese Operations have a slightly higher percentage of industrial products.

Geographical Performance: For the three months ended June 30, 2025, net sales increased by 2% in the Americas, 6% in Europe, and 4% in Asia/Rest of World in U.S. dollars. In local currencies, sales increased by 3% in both the Americas and Asia/Rest of World, and were flat in Europe. For the six months ended June 30, 2025, sales were flat in both the Americas and Asia/Rest of World, and decreased by 2% in Europe in U.S. dollars.

Sales Units: The company experienced strong growth in product inspection and process analytics within the U.S. Operations. However, the Chinese Operations saw a decline in laboratory-related products.

Future Outlook: The company is implementing various actions to mitigate the effects of tariffs, which have increased economic uncertainty in end markets and the global economic environment. The escalation in global trade disputes/tariffs may negatively impact gross margins during the remainder of 2025.

Segment Performance: The U.S. Operations saw a 3% increase in net sales to external customers for the three months ended June 30, 2025, driven by strong growth in product inspection and process analytics. The Swiss Operations experienced a 26% increase in segment profit for the same period, primarily due to higher net sales to other segments. The Western European Operations saw a 15% increase in segment profit, benefiting from increased net sales and margin expansion initiatives.

SEC Filing: METTLER TOLEDO INTERNATIONAL INC/ [ MTD ] - 10-Q - Aug. 01, 2025

data.title: Arthur J. Gallagher & Co. SEC 10-Q Report
data.description:
Arthur J. Gallagher & Co., a global leader in insurance brokerage, risk management, and consulting services, has released its latest Form 10-Q report, showcasing robust financial and operational performance for the recent quarter. The report highlights significant revenue growth, improved profitability, and strategic business expansions, underscoring the company's strong market position and future growth prospects.

Financial Highlights

  • Total Revenues: $3,220.8 million, reflecting a significant increase from the previous year's $2,775.4 million, driven by higher commissions and fees.
  • Earnings Before Income Taxes: $471.4 million, up from $365.6 million in the prior year, indicating improved operational efficiency.
  • Net Earnings: $366.2 million, compared to $285.4 million in the previous year, showcasing strong bottom-line growth.
  • Net Earnings Attributable to Controlling Interests: $365.8 million, up from $283.4 million, highlighting the company's effective cost management and revenue growth.
  • Basic Net Earnings Per Share: $1.43, an increase from $1.30, reflecting enhanced shareholder value.
  • Diluted Net Earnings Per Share: $1.40, up from $1.27, indicating improved profitability per share.

Business Highlights

  • Revenue Segments: Arthur J. Gallagher & Co. operates through three primary segments: Brokerage, Risk Management, and Corporate. The Brokerage segment provides insurance and reinsurance brokerage and consulting services, while the Risk Management segment offers contract claim settlement, claim administration, and risk management consulting services. The Corporate segment includes financial information related to debt, corporate costs, and foreign currency translation impacts.
  • Geographical Performance: The company has a significant presence in the United States, United Kingdom, Canada, Australia, New Zealand, and other foreign regions. The United States remains the largest market, with substantial goodwill allocated to this region, indicating strong operational performance. The United Kingdom and Australia also show significant contributions to the company's overall operations.
  • Business Combinations: During the six-month period ended June 30, 2025, Arthur J. data.title: U.S. Hiring Slowed in July, With 73,000 New Jobs — WSJ
data.description:
By Rachel Wolfe and Justin Lahart

U.S. job growth slowed in July, a signal that pockets of weakness that had been marring the labor market are starting to take hold.

The U.S. added a seasonally adjusted 73,000 jobs in July, the Labor Department reported Friday, below the gain of 100,000 jobs economists polled by The Wall Street Journal had expected to see.

Revisions cut down the jobs growth originally reported for May and June by a combined 258,000. That left May as having added just 19,000 jobs and June just 14,000.

The unemployment rate rose slightly to 4.2% from 4.1%. The number of people unemployed for 27 weeks or longer increased to 1.83 million from 1.65 million in June. That increase in the ranks of the long-term unemployed could be a reflection of an environment where layoffs have been low, but companies have been reluctant to hire, making it harder for people without work to find employment.

The healthcare and social-assistance sectors accounted for a large share of the new jobs. Those sectors tend to do well no matter how the economy is doing. Federal-government layoffs continued to drag on payrolls, with that sector losing 12,000 jobs.

Revisions have been consistently negative in 2025, with the Labor Department lowering its initially reported job count every month through June.

For June, the jobs numbers that were most notably much lower than originally reported were in state and local education.

When the June employment report was released a month ago, it had shown an increase of 63,500 state and local education jobs. Economists at the time said that was likely a fluky gain attributed to seasonal swings in school employment that the Labor Department can struggle to adjust for.

The revised figures took that June job gain down to 7,500.

The recent trend in downward revisions could reflect the fact that not all businesses respond to the Labor Department's monthly employer survey in time for the initial report. Those that do are more likely to be large, well-capitalized and seasoned. Later responders might be less equipped to handle challenges such as high tariffs and the sharp slowdown in the supply of immigrant labor. As their responses come in, the numbers get revised lower.

Stock futures traded lower early in the morning, after President Trump announced a raft of tariffs late Thursday. Stocks continued to fall Friday. The weak jobs report is likely to fuel bets on Federal Reserve rate cuts.

"The job market looks significantly worse today than it did before this report," said Jed Kolko, senior fellow at the Peterson Institute for International Economics.

Slow payroll growth hasn't kept up with those looking for jobs, he said, even as population growth has also slowed.

Wells Fargo senior economist Sarah House said July's report suggests that some of the fears about risks to the labor market that Fed Chair Jerome Powell highlighted at Wednesday's Fed meeting might be coming to fruition.

"There have been a lot of points of concern," said House. She pointed to a variety of factors. A huge share of hiring came from just a few industries. Average weekly hours worked remained relatively low. And people are remaining unemployed longer.

"We think things are only going to weaken from here," said House. "Yes, you're still adding jobs, but not everyone can work in healthcare or education."

Tariffs are a piece of the puzzle, she said, but not the only factor at play.

Guy Berger, senior fellow at labor-market think tank Burning Glass Institute, said he thinks the situation still may not be as bad as it looks on the surface.

The unemployment rate is basically where it was two months ago, he noted, even after such little new job creation. That tells him that only very low growth — or even no growth — is needed right now to keep the labor market treading water.

"It's not clear to me that this is any sort of turning point," said Berger. "It's an abnormal environment, but within an abnormal environment these are very normal numbers."

Still, Berger said, even without a surge in unemployment, low growth will create a less dynamic economy going forward — and make it harder for job seekers.

"It's miserable to be looking for a job right now," he said.

Write to Rachel Wolfe at rachel.wolfe@wsj.com and Justin Lahart at Justin.Lahart@wsj.com

data.title: Why Trump Keeps Fundraising Like a First-Term President — WSJ
data.description:
By Josh Dawsey, Anthony DeBarros and Rebecca Ballhaus

Donald Trump is amassing a bigger political war chest than any previous president, using his office to continually bring in donors and plotting how to avoid expected losses in next year's midterm elections.

The Trump-aligned MAGA Inc. super political-action committee raised $177 million in the first half of the year, according to its filing Thursday with the Federal Election Commission. That is just $33 million less than a prior super PAC supporting Trump hauled in during the second half of 2024, when he was in the middle of his campaign. Meanwhile, a web of Trump fundraising committees, including his leadership PAC Never Surrender and the Republican National Committee, ended June with more than $350 million in cash — in line with totals during the election.

Trump's political operation has amassed commitments for $1.4 billion, according to a person familiar with the matter — roughly the same amount as contributions in 2024 to Trump's committees, super PAC and the Republican Party.

Part of the haul has been the result of email solicitations to small-dollar donors in which he has repeatedly offered the prospect of perks, including having their names hung on the wall of the Oval Office. One July missive promised donors the title of "Trump White House Advisor" for those who completed a poll.

Millions of dollars also have flowed from fundraisers Trump continues to hold, often priced at more than $1 million to attend. They are usually held at his Mar-a-Lago resort in Florida and his golf club in Bedminster, NJ, where he is often flanked by Cabinet and senior administration officials. One dinner is scheduled for Friday evening, according to an invitation reviewed by The Wall Street Journal.

Most presidents after winning re-election stop aggressive fundraising, according to strategists in both parties. Trump, however, has been determined to keep raising historic sums of money, according to people who have spoken to him. He has told advisers that if Democrats win the House of Representatives, they will investigate and try to impeach him, and he doesn't want his last two years to be miserable.

Several people involved in the discussions said the money was expected to be spent on promoting Trump's favored candidates, protecting vulnerable members of Congress and attacking Democrats. Some Trump advisers privately said they would need to spend considerable money to promote elements of Trump's "Big, Beautiful" tax law because it is unpopular with swaths of the country. Trump's team is planning to focus on individual elements of the law they say polling shows are more popular, such as no taxes on tips or overtime. Trump's team is planning to dispatch Cabinet officials across the country this fall to promote the legislation, advisers said.

"The President's priorities in the One Big Beautiful Bill are hugely popular among ALL Americans, including: no tax on tips, no tax on overtime, able-bodied work requirements to qualify for Medicaid, tax cuts for middle income families," said Danielle Alvarez, a spokeswoman for the RNC.

The funds being raised could help shore up candidates in tight races, though inflation or an unpopular war could drag Republicans down next year, said Karl Rove, a prominent Republican strategist.

"It's always better to be in a position where you have resources," he said.

Among the key figures in Trump's 2026 political operation are White House officials Susie Wiles and James Blair, outside advisers Chris LaCivita and Tony Fabrizio, along with fundraiser Meredith O'Rourke and officials at the RNC. His team has installed allies at the Senate Leadership Fund and the House-focused Congressional Leadership Fund. Advisers have met at the White House and at Camp David to discuss strategy ahead of the midterms, according to people familiar with the meetings.

Besides staging the major-donor dinners at his club, Trump's team continues to cultivate online donors as no president has done before and in a way that could exhaust some of those email recipients, GOP strategists said.

An adviser familiar with the operation said in some weeks the emails can generate a few million dollars. To make the pitches more lucrative, the team has tried to shape them around what Trump is doing as president, the adviser said. One pitch sought to raise money on Trump's announcing the June cease-fire between Israel and Iran.

"Would you like a job for the White House?" read another. Those who give $35 or more are promised a White House badge, according to that email.

Other solicitations dangle a DOGE membership card, a copy of an executive order and a poll on whether Trump should hang an "OFFICIAL DONOR LIST" in the Oval Office, which he hasn't done, according to officials.

In some, he has offered Trump-branded merchandise. In June, the day after NATO Secretary-General Mark Rutte referred to Trump as "daddy," comparing the Iran-Israel situation to disciplining children, another plea landed in Trump donor inboxes, with an offer, for $35, of a T-shirt with his face and the word "DADDY."

His team has sent more than 700 such email pitches — or about five a day — since taking office, a review by The Wall Street Journal shows.

According to disclosures included in the emails, the money is divided across Trump's Never Surrender PAC, as well as the RNC and a group that has ties to Vice President JD Vance, called Working for Ohio.

The leadership PAC, which gives Trump broad sway in how he can spend the money, didn't respond to a request for comment.

Trump's instinct for preserving the GOP majority might only go so far, and Trump is considering targeting some fellow Republicans, including Kentucky Rep. Thomas Massie, whom he disdains because Massie has criticized him and voted against his priorities at times.

The Trump team thinks it can use the extensive trove of data collected on Trump voters to drive up turnout in the midterm elections, which are traditionally low. The president himself is expected to approve most of the spending, advisers said.

Trump had struggled in the past to get voters to show up in elections when he wasn't on the ballot, including in the 2018 and 2022 midterm elections when Republicans underperformed, said David Axelrod, a prominent Democratic strategist.

This time, he said, the money is essential to having a chance. "He understands the odds are not with him, and I expect he will go at it with full fury and desperation," Axelrod said. "The question is can he change history here?"

Write to Josh Dawsey at Joshua.Dawsey@WSJ.com, Anthony DeBarros at anthony.debarros@wsj.com and Rebecca Ballhaus at rebecca.ballhaus@wsj.com

data.title: Week's Best: Margin Debt Hits Record in June — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Barron's Advisor Staff

Margin trading was hot in June. Debt balances in investors' margin accounts topped $1 trillion that month, a record, according to data from Finra, the brokerage industry's self-regulatory organization. The rapid acceleration in investors' use of margin debt calls to mind the meme stock frenzy and dot-com bubble.

Among other most-read wealth management articles this week:

Stock-picking odds are the pits . Only a tiny fraction of stocks beat the return of a broad index over time. Columnist Allan Roth confesses that as much as he likes watching financial shows, he also understands the dismal odds of picking stocks that will beat the market. He shares academic research that drives that point home.

Protecting clients against senior scams . Financial exploitation of the elderly is nothing new, but familiar scams have become digitized, putting seniors at greater risk, says Adam Frank, head of wealth planning and advice at J.P. Morgan Wealth Management. Using fake caller IDs and delivering increasingly legitimate-looking emails, scammers are harnessing modern technology for nefarious ends, says Frank. "The rise of AI is, for me, the most frightening aspect of all of this," he says.

Problematic profit-sharing plan . Regulators have suspended a former Schwab advisor over allegations that he formed an illicit profit-sharing arrangement with a client who was also a family member. Daniel Roper allegedly lost money while executing more than 20,000 trades in a little more than a year in an account he didn't have discretion over. He accepted a two-year suspension and agreed to a $15,000 fine and to pay nearly $81,000 in disgorgement, according to a letter detailing the settlement that was published by brokerage industry self-regulator Finra.

How to leave a family practice. For many young advisors, going into business with a family member is a logical career step, but it can lead to hard feelings if the arrangement doesn't work. Even previously close relationships can be permanently impaired because of a professional falling out, especially if lawyers are involved. Advisors who have parted professionally with family members say open communication is the key to avoiding drama.

What you need to know about AI. Financial advisors who ignore artificial intelligence do so at their peril. A recent industry survey found the adoption of AI by wealth management firms is over 40%, and 25% are actively exploring the technology. Three things practice leaders need to know: Employees need training in using AI to unlock potential productivity gains; you need to establish AI ethics and compliance guidelines; and it's imperative to protect against cybersecurity threats and data breaches. Our columnist includes more advice and helpful resources.

data.title: Trump Orders Firing of Labor Department Official After Weak Jobs Report — WSJ
data.description:
By Caitlin McCabe and Roshan Fernandez

President Trump said he directed his team to fire the top Bureau of Labor Statistics official after the bureau issued a weak jobs report on Friday.

​The report weighed on markets Friday along with Trump's revamped tariff plan. Trump in a social media post said Erika McEntarfer, the BLS commissioner, would be "replaced with someone much more competent and qualified," asserting without evidence that the government's jobs numbers have been manipulated for political purposes.

The July jobs report undershot forecasts, suggesting the economy added just 106,000 positions over the past three months, although unemployment stood at 4.2% last month, matching expectations.

The dollar sank, while Treasury yields dropped and the Dow industrials shed more than 1%. The VIX, a market-volatility index often dubbed a fear gauge, shot higher. After the report, investors ramped up bets that the Federal Reserve will now be more inclined to cut rates to support the economy.

On tariffs, trading partners, including Switzerland and Taiwan, said they would keep trying to negotiate with the White House. Switzerland faces a 39% tariff rate-higher than the 31% floated in April-while Taiwanese goods will be levied at a 20% rate.

The president hiked levies on Canadian goods that don't comply with a North American trade agreement to 35%, starting today. Prime Minister Mark Carney said that he was disappointed and Canada had already moved to address concerns over fentanyl.

Countries that run small trade surpluses with the U.S. will largely get 15% duties, while those with deficits will have 10% tariffs. Trump gave Mexico more time to set a trade deal and avoid higher tariffs, extending current rates by 90 days.

Amazon shares sold off after the company reported disappointing growth in its cloud-computing arm, while investors dug into earnings from Chevron, Exxon Mobil and Colgate-Palmolive.

By the end of Friday, around one-third of S&P 500 members will have reported this week. Companies are warning that tariff uncertainty is weighing on their businesses.

In recent trading:

Stock indexes dropped, with the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite falling more than 2%, leading losses. The Dow faced its worst week since early April.

Treasury yields fell after the jobs report. Yields on 10-year notes dropped more than 0.1 percentage point.

European stocks skidded, with Italian, French and German indexes down more than 2.5%. Switzerland's stock market was shut for a holiday.

Asian indexes declined too. South Korea's Kospi slid 3.9% after Seoul proposed higher taxes on corporations and investors.

The WSJ Dollar Index handed back earlier gains, shedding about 1%.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Led Zeppelin's Jimmy Page, Sony Pictures settle songwriter's 'Dazed and Confused' lawsuit
data.description:
Songwriter Jake Holmes has settled a lawsuit that accused former Led Zeppelin guitarist Jimmy Page, music publisher Warner Chappell and film studio Sony Pictures of violating his rights in the song "Dazed and Confused," according to a Friday filing in California federal court.

The parties told the court that they had resolved their dispute and were finalizing a written settlement. Details of the settlement agreement were not immediately available.

A spokesperson for Warner Music and Holmes' attorney Daniel Johnson declined to comment. Spokespeople for Sony Pictures did not immediately respond to a request for comment or more information about the settlement.

Holmes, a singer-songwriter and prominent creator of advertising jingles, wrote "Dazed and Confused" in 1967. Page's band, the Yardbirds, reworked it later that year, and his next band, Led Zeppelin, featured it on their debut album in 1969.

Holmes first sued Page for copyright infringement in 2010 over his versions of the song. That dispute was settled in 2011.

Holmes filed a new lawsuit in May alleging that Page released several archival Yardbirds live versions of "Dazed and Confused" that only credit Page as the song's writer, breaking their settlement agreement and infringing Holmes' copyright. The complaint said that the Sony Pictures documentary "Becoming Led Zeppelin," released in February also unlawfully featured "Dazed and Confused" without compensating or crediting Holmes.

Page, Sony Pictures and Warner Chappell did not respond to the allegations in court.

The case is Holmes v. Page, U.S. District Court for the Central District of California, No. 2:25-cv-03977.

For Holmes: Daniel Johnson

For the defendants: attorney information not available

Read more:

Jimmy Page, Sony Pictures sued by songwriter over Led Zeppelin song

data.title: Construction costs rise at Canada's Woodfibre LNG project
data.description:
Construction costs at Canada's Woodfibre LNG project have increased, driving up capital costs for all partners involved, Canadian pipeline company Enbridge reported on Friday.

The Woodfibre LNG project is a 2.1-million tonne liquefied natural gas export facility under construction near Squamish, British Columbia. The project is one of several new LNG facilities planned for Canada's Pacific coast, and is expected to be complete in 2027.

The project's capital cost was initially estimated at US$5.1 billion. But Enbridge, which owns a 30% stake in the project, said Friday on a conference call that costs have recently increased due to permit delays, building code changes, a second floating hotel to accommodate workers, and challenging on-site conditions.

"Our share of the project costs have increased from US$1.5 billion to US$2.9 billion, and our partners' proportionate share has increased similarly," an Enbridge spokesperson said in an email.

The 70% remaining stake in the Woodfibre project is owned by Pacific Energy Corp Ltd, which is part of the Singapore-based RGE Group of companies. Woodfibre LNG did not immediately respond to a request for comment Friday.

Enbridge said Friday it is still expecting low double-digit returns from the project, relatively consistent with what it had initially expected. The company remains excited about the project and the LNG market, Enbridge's spokesperson said.

data.title: WORLD KINECT CORP SEC 10-Q Report
data.description:
World Kinect Corp, a global leader in energy logistics and services, has released its Form 10-Q report for the second quarter of 2025. The report outlines significant financial and operational developments, reflecting the company's ongoing efforts to navigate a challenging economic environment and implement strategic restructuring initiatives.

Financial Highlights

Revenue: $9,043.3 million, reflecting a decrease from $10,965.2 million in the prior year period, primarily due to lower sales volumes across segments.

Gross Profit: $232.4 million, down from $245.2 million, indicating a slight decline in profitability despite cost management efforts.

Operating Income (loss): $(345.1) million, compared to $45.2 million in the previous year, largely impacted by goodwill and other asset impairments.

Net Income (loss) attributable to World Kinect: $(339.4) million, a significant decrease from $108.3 million, driven by restructuring charges and asset impairments.

Basic earnings (loss) per common share: $(6.06), compared to $1.81, reflecting the impact of lower net income and share repurchases.

Business Highlights

Aviation Segment Performance

The aviation segment has shown growth in fuel and related service offerings, enhanced logistics capabilities, and geographic expansion into additional international airport locations. The segment has benefited from higher returns in a high interest rate environment due to improvements in working capital management.

Land Segment Restructuring

The land segment is undergoing significant restructuring to improve capital efficiency and optimize asset utilization. This includes the sale of the U.K. land fuels business and a reassessment of business lines to focus on core activities with the highest return potential.

Marine Segment Stability

The marine segment continues to perform well, benefiting from elevated fuel prices and volatility, as well as a constrained credit environment. The segment is positioned to generate moderate earnings in stable markets and additional value in volatile markets.

Geographical Performance

  • Asia Pacific: Reported revenues of $1,094.2 million for the three months ended June 30, 2025, reflecting a decrease compared to the previous year, indicating challenges in this market.
  • EMEA: Generated $1,926.4 million in revenue for the three months ended June 30, 2025, showing a decline from the previous year, which may be attributed to macroeconomic pressures.
  • LATAM: Reported $978.5 million in revenue for the three months ended June 30, 2025, also experiencing a decrease from the prior year, suggesting regional economic challenges.
  • North America: Remains a strong market with $5,050.7 million in revenue for the three months ended June 30, 2025, although this represents a decline from the previous year, possibly due to restructuring activities.

Future Outlook

Restructuring and Cost Savings: The company is implementing a 2025 Restructuring Plan aimed at streamlining operations and enhancing efficiency. Expected cost savings from these initiatives are projected to be approximately $80 million over the five-year period from 2026 through 2030.

Global Finance Optimization: A program to optimize global finance and accounting operations is underway, with completion expected by the fourth quarter of 2026. This initiative is anticipated to result in initial cost savings beginning in 2026, with increased savings in subsequent years.

data.title: HOST HOTELS & RESORTS, INC. SEC 10-Q Report
data.description:
Host Hotels & Resorts, Inc., a leading lodging real estate investment trust, has released its Form 10-Q report for the second quarter of 2025. The report highlights the company's financial performance and operational activities, providing insights into its revenue growth, profitability, and business strategies amidst a dynamic market environment.

Financial Highlights

Total Revenues: $1,586 million, increased by 8.2% for the second quarter of 2025 compared to 2024, driven by a 3.7% increase in room rates at comparable hotels and strong transient demand.

Operating Profit: $277 million, decreased by 5.1% for the second quarter of 2025 compared to 2024, primarily due to a decrease in net gains on insurance settlements.

Net Income: $225 million, decreased by 7.0% for the second quarter of 2025 compared to 2024, impacted by declines in net gains on insurance settlements and increases in depreciation, interest expense, and income taxes.

Diluted Earnings Per Common Share: $0.32, decreased by 5.9% for the second quarter of 2025 compared to 2024, reflecting the changes in net income.

Business Highlights

Revenue Segments

The company reported an increase in total revenues driven by a 3.7% increase in room rates at comparable hotels, primarily due to strong transient demand. Additionally, food and beverage revenues and ancillary spend contributed to revenue growth.

Geographical Performance

The Miami, Maui, and San Francisco/San Jose markets showed significant growth in Total RevPAR, with increases of 16.4%, 18.5%, and 15.7% respectively, driven by rate growth and strong transient demand. Conversely, the Austin and Washington, D.C. markets experienced declines due to renovation projects and convention center closures.

Sales Units

Transient business accounted for approximately 60% of room sales, with transient revenue increasing by 6.8% for the quarter, reflecting strong demand and improving leisure demand on Maui. Group business, which accounted for 36% of room sales, saw a decrease in revenue by 4.9% due to renovation disruptions and a shift from group to transient business.

Future Outlook

The company anticipates that demand will continue to be driven by transient business, with expected softness in short-term group bookings due to economic uncertainties. Comparable hotel RevPAR growth for the full year 2025 is expected to be between 1.5% and 2.5%, with continued recovery at Maui properties.

Operational Challenges

The company faced challenges such as planned renovation disruptions and a shift in business mix from group to transient in Maui, impacting group demand. Additionally, high interest rates, geopolitical instability, and labor market conditions pose risks to economic growth.

New Production Launches

The company continued construction on the development of 40 condominiums at the Four Seasons Resort Orlando, expected to be completed in the fourth quarter of 2025, with villas expected in the first half of 2026.

  • Income Tax Expense: $15.08 million for the three-months ended June 30, 2025, compared to $11.16 million in the same period of 2024, reflecting higher pre-tax earnings.

Business Highlights

  • Revenue Segments: The company's primary source of revenue is providing loans and banking services to consumers and commercial customers in Texas. Additionally, the company owns First Financial Trust & Asset Management Company, First Financial Insurance Agency, Inc. (inactive), First Technology Services, Inc., FFB Investment Paris Fund, LLC, and FFB Portfolio Management, Inc., which contribute to its revenue streams.
  • Geographical Performance: The company operates 79 locations in Texas, indicating a strong regional focus. The performance of these locations is crucial to the company's overall business operations, as they serve as the primary market for its banking and financial services.
  • Sales Units: The company reported a total of $8,074,944,000 in loans held-for-investment as of June 30, 2025, with significant contributions from commercial, agricultural, real estate, and consumer segments. This reflects the company's operational scale in terms of loan issuance.
  • Future Outlook: The company continues to focus on its core banking operations in Texas, with an emphasis on maintaining strong credit quality and managing its loan portfolio effectively. The extension of the stock repurchase authorization through July 31, 2026, indicates a commitment to returning value to shareholders.

data.title: American Assets Trust, Inc. SEC 10-Q Report
data.description:
American Assets Trust, Inc., a prominent real estate investment trust (REIT) specializing in retail, office, multifamily, and mixed-use properties, has released its Form 10-Q report for the first quarter of 2025. The report underscores the company's stable financial performance and strategic operational initiatives amidst fluctuating market conditions.

Financial Highlights

  • Total Revenue: $216.54 million, reflecting a slight decrease compared to the previous period, indicating stable performance amidst market conditions.
  • Operating Income: $97.95 million, showing a significant increase due to a gain on the sale of real estate, highlighting effective asset management.
  • Net Income: $61.23 million, demonstrating strong profitability with a notable gain from asset sales contributing to the bottom line.
  • Earnings Per Common Share, Basic: $0.79, indicating a solid return for shareholders, supported by strategic financial management.
  • Earnings Per Common Share, Diluted: $0.79, consistent with basic EPS, reflecting stable share performance and effective capital structure management.

Business Highlights

  • Segment Performance: The company operates in four reportable business segments: retail real estate, office real estate, multifamily real estate, and mixed-use real estate. Each segment includes rental and tenant services, with the mixed-use segment also operating a 369-room all-suite hotel.
  • Retail Segment: The retail segment primarily includes rental of retail space and other tenant services, such as tenant reimbursements, parking, and storage space rental.
  • Office Segment: The office segment focuses on rental of office space and similar tenant services, including tenant reimbursements and parking.
  • Multifamily Segment: This segment includes rental of apartments and other tenant services, contributing to the company's diversified real estate portfolio.
  • Mixed-Use Segment: The mixed-use segment combines retail space rental with hotel operations, providing a unique blend of services and revenue streams.
  • Geographical Performance: The company's properties are concentrated in Southern California, Northern California, Washington, Oregon, Texas, and Hawaii, with a significant portion located in Southern California, exposing the company to regional economic risks.
  • Tenant Industry Concentration: Tenants in the office industry accounted for 47.0% of total revenues, while tenants in the retail industry accounted for 22.0%, indicating a strong reliance on these sectors.
  • New Acquisitions: On February 28, 2025, the company acquired Genesee Park in San Diego, California, a 192-unit apartment community, for $67.9 million, enhancing its multifamily segment.
  • Property Dispositions: The company sold Del Monte Center in Monterey, California, on February 25, 2025, for $123.5 million, reflecting strategic portfolio management.
  • Future Outlook: The company continues to focus on strategic acquisitions and dispositions to optimize its real estate portfolio and enhance shareholder value.

data.title: Law firm Quinn Emanuel accused of turning on ex-client in fintech case
data.description:
A financial technology provider previously represented by Quinn Emanuel accused the law firm of betraying its loyalties and violating ethics rules by taking the case of a former executive suing the company over his termination.

In a filing in the case in California federal court on Thursday, Pagaya Technologies asked a judge to disqualify Quinn Emanuel from representing Hugh Edmundson in the lawsuit he filed in June.

In the new filing Pagaya and its hedge fund affiliate Theorem Technology accused Quinn Emanuel of breaking attorney ethics rules in its new work for Edmundson, a former top executive at Theorem. Pagaya had acquired Theorem last year.

“The firm is a repeat offender, having been disqualified multiple times for this exact misconduct,” lawyers for Pagaya and Theorem at law firm Gibson Dunn wrote. Pagaya said Quinn Emanuel had “brazenly violated its ethical obligations by turning around and suing its own client.”

Quinn Emanuel and a lawyer for Edmundson did not immediately respond to requests for comment.

In a statement on Friday, a spokesperson for law firm Gibson Dunn said "Quinn Emanuel is suing its former client over matters about which it previously advised — a clear conflict of interest that courts have not hesitated to disqualify firms for in the past."

Last year, Quinn Emanuel was disqualified from representing Elon Musk's social media platform X in a lawsuit accusing data-scraping company Bright Data of illegally copying and selling content. The law firm had previously represented Bright Data in another lawsuit.

In an April letter from Quinn Emanuel to Edmundson's attorneys, which was included in the court file, the firm had said the "suggestion of a conflict on the part of Quinn Emanuel is false and inaccurate."

The firm also said it had set up "appropriate ethical screens out of an abundance of caution" to separate its prior work for Pagaya with its representation of Edmundson.

Edmundson's lawsuit accused Pagaya and Theorem of wrongful termination, retaliation and other claims stemming from his firing in April from Theorem, where he was chief investment officer. Edmundson founded Theorem in 2014.

Edmundson alleged that “almost immediately after the merger closed, defendants began systematically dismantling Theorem’s independence and strategic decision-making authority.”

Quinn Emanuel provided legal work last year to Pagaya on non-compete and non-solicitation provisions related to Theorem employees, Pagaya said.

It further alleged that Quinn Emanuel's prior work for the company has created a "direct and incurable conflict of interest."

In Thursday’s court filing, Pagaya said that Edmundson’s lawsuit was baseless and that its claims are governed by a binding arbitration agreement.

The case is Hugh Edmundson v. Pagaya Technologies et al, U.S. District Court, Northern District of California, No

For plaintiff: Diane Doolittle and Michael Liftik of Quinn Emanuel

For defendants: Brian Ascher, Katherine Smith and George Adams III of Gibson, Dunn & Crutcher

data.title: Levi & Korsinsky Investigates Possible Securities Fraud by Simulations Plus, Inc. (SLP)
data.description:
NEW YORK, NY / ACCESS Newswire / August 1, 2025 / Levi & Korsinsky notifies investors that it has commenced an investigation of Simulations Plus, Inc. ("Simulations Plus, Inc.") concerning possible violations of federal securities laws.

Simulations Plus reported its financial results for its third fiscal quarter on July 14, 2025. Among other items, Simulations Plus reported sales of $20.4 million, missing the consensus estimate of $20.9 million. Simulations Plus also reported a net loss of $67.3 million and diluted loss per share of $3.35, reflecting a non-cash impairment charge of $77.2 million, compared to net income of $3.1 million and diluted earnings per share of $0.15 for the same period in 2024. The Company also revised its fiscal 2025 adjusted earnings guidance to $0.93 to $1.06, down from the previous guidance of $1.07 to $1.20. Simulations Plus advised investors that, in June, the Company had initiated a restructuring of its operations, including workforce reductions and cost-cutting measures, aimed at improving operational efficiency and reducing expenses.

Following this news, Simulations Plus's stock price fell over 25% on July 15, 2025. To obtain additional information, go to:
